1: The fifth angel sounds; a fallen star is given the key to the bottomless pit. Christ holds the keys of Death and Hades (Revelation 1:18) and the key of David (Revelation 3:7). The key given to this fallen star is delegated authority — the Abyss is not opened by any autonomous power but by divine permission under Christ's governance. The one who was given all authority in heaven and on earth (Matthew 28:18) permits the opening of the Abyss for the purpose of his judicial plans.
2: Smoke pours from the Abyss darkening the sun and air — demonic forces can only operate by obscuring what Christ illumines. Christ is the 'light of the world' (John 8:12; Revelation 9:5); the sun's darkening is the temporary obscuring of created light by unleashed demonic darkness. The darkness cannot ultimately overcome the light (John 1:5), but during the permitted period of demonic activity the light's fullness is dimmed — not extinguished.
3: Demonic locusts emerge from the smoke — the eighth plague of Egypt (Exodus 10:12-20) redeployed as an eschatological judgment. Christ executes a new-Exodus reckoning through the permitted activity of demonic forces. The locusts' scorpion-power is a limited, constrained power — like the Satan of Job 1-2, they afflict within the limits set by the one who holds the keys to their prison.
4: The locusts cannot harm those with the seal of God — the Christological seal of the Holy Spirit (Revelation 7:2-3; Ephesians 1:13; Revelation 4:30) protects Christ's own from demonic assault. This is the Passover principle applied eschatologically: the blood of the Lamb (the atoning death of Christ) marks his people as his possession, and the destroying power must pass over them (Exodus 12:13). Christ's cross is the boundary that demonic power cannot cross for those who are in him.
5: The locusts can torment but not kill, for five months — the limit of demonic power is set by divine decree. Job 1:12 ('on the man himself do not lay a finger') is the prototype: Satan afflicts within the limits Christ sets. The 'five months' is the life-span of a locust — the judgment is seasonal, bounded, not eternal. Christ's governance of demonic activity is precise.
6: 'People will seek death and not find it; they will long to die, and death will flee from them.' Christ promised: 'I came that they may have life and have it abundantly' (John 10:10). The demonic torment produces the opposite — desperate desire for death, the complete inversion of the life Christ offers. Death itself, which Christ holds (Revelation 1:18), refuses to grant the merciful release its seekers desperately want. This is the eschatological consequence of rejecting the gift of life that Christ alone provides.
7: The locusts' appearance — like war horses with gold crowns, human faces, and lion teeth — creates a grotesque parody of the divine throne-room. The four living creatures of Revelation 4 represent the fullness of creation in worship before God; these demonic creatures parody that fullness in destruction. The demonic always imitates the divine but in distorted, death-dealing form — Christ's true image (the firstborn of all creation, Colossians 1:15) is counterfeited by the demonic, which imitates without creating.
8: 'They had hair like women's hair and teeth like lions' teeth.' The composite description defies natural category — these are creatures belonging to no created order but to the chaos that Christ has conquered. At the cross, Christ 'disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15). The locust-army's terrifying appearance is the appearance of a defeated power — frightening precisely because it knows its time is limited (Revelation 12:12).
9: Breastplates like iron, wings like charging chariots — the imagery of overwhelming force against which the communities of the seven cities are humanly powerless. But Christ's promise to his church is not military capacity but sealing and protection: 'Fear not, for I have overcome the world' (John 16:33). The iron breastplates of the demonic locusts are answered not by the church's armor but by the Lamb's blood, which has already won the definitive battle.
10: Scorpion-tails with stingers causing five months of torment — the bounded power of the demonic again emphasized. Luke 10:19: Jesus said 'I have given you authority to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy; and nothing shall hurt you.' The authority Christ grants to his messengers to tread on scorpions exceeds the scorpion-power of the locusts. The demonic authority is over-matched by the Christological authority delegated to the church.
11: Their king is the angel of the Abyss — Abaddon in Hebrew, Apollyon in Greek (the Destroyer). Christ is the Prince of Peace (Isaiah 9:6) and the one who 'destroys the works of the devil' (1 John 3:8). The Destroyer-king parodies and inverts the Prince of Peace: the satanic kingdom's leadership is defined by destruction; Christ's kingdom by the creation and sustaining of life. Apollyon is a created angel operating in the Abyss that Christ holds the key to open and close.
12: 'The first woe has passed; behold, two woes are still to come.' The staged structure of Christ's judgment: the woes escalate but are not simultaneous. Divine patience is built into the structure — there are pauses between the seals, between the trumpets, between the bowls. Each stage gives opportunity for repentance (Revelation 9:20-21). Christ's judgment is not hasty; the pauses reflect the longsuffering of the one who is 'not wishing that any should perish' (2 Peter 3:9).
13: The sixth angel sounds; a voice from the four horns of the golden altar commands the release of four bound angels. The voice comes from the altar — the same altar of incense (Revelation 8:3-5) where the prayers of the saints were offered. Judgment proceeds from the same place as intercession: the altar that held the martyrs' prayers (Revelation 6:9) and the saints' intercessions (Revelation 8:3) now issues the command for the next wave of judgment. Christ's intercession and his judgment are inseparable functions of the same high-priestly authority.
14: 'Release the four angels who are bound at the great river Euphrates.' The 'bound' angels are constrained by divine decree — they operate only at the appointed time. The language of 'bound and released' echoes Jesus's teaching on binding and loosing (Matthew 16:19; Revelation 18:18): what is bound or released in the divine plan is under the authority of the one who holds the keys to the kingdom.
15: 'The four angels who had been prepared for this very hour, day, month, and year.' The divine precision — hour, day, month, year — is the precision of sovereign providence. Christ said: 'It is not for you to know times or seasons that the Father has fixed by his own authority' (Acts 1:7); but those times and seasons are fixed. The judgment at the exact appointed moment demonstrates that history's events are under Christ's governance at the level of individual hours.
16: Two hundred million mounted troops — the numerical scale staggers the imagination but does not escape Christ's governance. He who is 'ruler of the kings of the earth' (Revelation 1:5) rules armies of this magnitude. The scale emphasizes the cosmic dimension of the judgment; the limitation to 'kill a third of humanity' (Revelation 9:15) emphasizes that even two hundred million troops operate within divinely set bounds. Magnitude does not equal autonomy from Christ's sovereignty.
17: The horses breathe fire, smoke, and sulfur — the plagues of Sodom (Genesis 19:24: fire and sulfur) and the lake of fire (Revelation 20:10) are anticipated in miniature. The demonic cavalry employs the instruments of final judgment in a penultimate form — warning and foretaste of what awaits those who do not repent. Christ's parables described similar warnings (Matthew 13:40-42: the fiery furnace); these trumpet plagues are parables acted out on a cosmic scale.
18: A third of humanity killed by the three plagues — fire, smoke, sulfur. The fractional measure continues the pattern of proportioned, not total, judgment. These judgments are warnings before the final reckoning (which comes with the bowls of wrath in Revelation 15-16). Christ's judgment is pedagogical as well as penal: the escalation from seals to trumpets to bowls mirrors the escalation of warnings before the final settling of accounts.
19: The horses' mouths and tails both cause harm — the dual weapon echoes the serpent's tail (Revelation 12:4) and the locusts' tails (Revelation 9:10). The demonic powers work through speech (lies from the mouth, John 8:44: the devil is the father of lies) and through historical action (the tail, representing deceptions that still harm). Christ defeats both dimensions: his word (John 8:32: 'the truth will set you free') and his historical act of the cross answer the demonic mouth and tail.
20: 'The rest of mankind did not repent of the works of their hands and stop worshipping demons.' The trumpet judgments — like the Exodus plagues that demanded Pharaoh release God's people — aimed to call to repentance. The response is hardness. The idols of gold, silver, bronze, stone, and wood (Psalms 115:4-8; Daniel 5:23) cannot see, hear, or walk — yet they receive worship while the living God is ignored. The refusal to repent after such judgments underscores that judgment alone cannot produce what Christ's atonement is uniquely able to produce: genuine transformation of the heart (2 Corinthians 7:10).
21: 'They did not repent of their murders, their sorceries, their sexual immorality, or their thefts.' The four specific sins cluster around the pagan religious and commercial practices of the seven-cities culture: sorcery (pharmakeia) common in temple contexts; sexual immorality integral to temple cult and guild banquets; murder encompassing the execution of believers; theft naming economic exploitation. The repeated absence of repentance demonstrates that judgment alone cannot produce what Christ's cross uniquely accomplishes: the redemption that calls the heart to genuine repentance.
